perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, without regard to the conflicts of law principles thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to the conflicts of law principles thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from New York to Delaware introduces potential legal risk. While both states have sophisticated corporate law, certain consumer protection statutes, particularly regarding data breach notification or implied warranties, may differ. This change omits the specific consumer protections afforded under New York law, potentially disadvantaging the Company if the Possible Transaction impacts New York consumers.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GBL § 889-a mandates specific data breach notification requirements in New York. By changing the governing law to Delaware, the agreement potentially avoids these specific requirements, weakening data breach protection for New York residents whose information might be involved in the transaction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation and law explanation indicate that changing the governing law from New York to Delaware could weaken data breach protection for New York residents, as NY GBL § 889-a mandates specific data breach notification requirements. The changed text, by omitting the application of New York law, potentially circumvents these specific requirements. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that NYSenate.gov is the official source for New York legislation, suggesting the existence of legally mandated requirements like data breach notification, which are being omitted. This omission creates a legal contradiction as it removes legally mandated consumer protection, which is in line with the 'Omission Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: We understand and agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by us or our representatives and that the Company will be entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief as remedies for any such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement by us or our representatives but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity.
perturbation.changed_text: We understand and agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by us or our representatives and that the Company will be entitled to injunctive relief as remedies for any such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement by us or our representatives but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'specific performance' as a remedy may violate established legal precedents regarding the availability of specific performance in contracts involving unique assets or confidential information. New York law generally allows specific performance when monetary damages are inadequate. Omitting it could limit the Company's ability to compel Carpenter Technology to fulfill its obligations under the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY UCC § 2-716 allows for specific performance when the goods are unique or in other proper circumstances.  While this agreement isn't explicitly about the sale of goods, the unique nature of the confidential information and potential business opportunity related to Talley Industries could justify specific performance.  Removing this remedy limits the Company's options in case of a breach, potentially contradicting the spirit, if not the direct letter, of the law allowing specific performance in situations where monetary damages are insufficient.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 38 Uniform Commercial Code ARTICLE 2 Sales PART 7 Remedies previous SECTION 2-715 Buyer's Incidental and Consequential Damages up PART 7 Remedies next SECTION 2-717 Deduction of Damages From the Price This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2-716 Buyer's Right to Specific Performance or Replevin Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 2, PART 7 Section 2--716. Buyer's Right to Specific Performance or Replevin. (1) Specific performance may be decreed where the goods are unique or in other proper circumstances. (2) The decree for specific performance may include such terms and conditions as to payment of the price, damages, or other relief as the court may deem just. (3) The buyer has a right of replevin for goods identified to the contract if after reasonable effort he is unable to effect cover for such goods or the circumstances reasonably indicate that such effort will be unavailing or if the goods have been shipped under reservation and satisfaction of the security interest in them has been made or tendered. In the case of goods bought for personal, family or household purposes, the buyer's right of replevin vests upon acquisition of a special property, even if the seller had not then repudiated or failed to deliver.

perturbation.justification: The original text allows for both specific performance and injunctive relief, while the changed text omits specific performance as a remedy. The scraped law snippets, specifically NY UCC § 2-716, state that "Specific performance may be decreed where the goods are unique or in other proper circumstances." The removal of specific performance as a remedy directly contradicts this law, particularly if the circumstances involve unique goods or opportunities, thus limiting the company's recourse in a breach and creating a significant legal contradiction. The Perturbation and Law Explanations highlight the importance of specific performance when monetary damages are insufficient, further underscoring the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: We will be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by our representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: We will be responsible for any material breach of this Agreement by our representatives.
perturbation.explanation: Adding the word 'material' before 'breach' introduces ambiguity and weakens Carpenter Technology's liability for its representatives' actions. New York law generally holds principals liable for all breaches by their agents within the scope of their authority, regardless of materiality. This change could allow Carpenter Technology to avoid liability for minor but potentially damaging breaches by its representatives.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York law of agency;  Maurillo v. Park Slope Urologists, 2017 NY Slip Op 08170
perturbation.law_citation: Maurillo v. Park Slope Urologists, 2017 NY Slip Op 08170
perturbation.law_explanation: New York agency law holds principals liable for the actions of their agents (representatives) acting within the scope of their authority. While this isn't codified in a single statute, the case law clearly establishes this principle.  Adding 'material' introduces a subjective standard that could allow Carpenter to avoid liability for breaches committed by its representatives that it deems not 'material,' which contradicts the broader principles of agency law.
perturbation.justification: The original contract states that 'We will be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by our representatives'. The modified contract introduces the word 'material', which changes the clause to: 'We will be responsible for any material breach of this Agreement by our representatives'. The Perturbation Explanation clarifies that this change weakens liability. The Law Explanation supports the notion that principals are liable for the actions of their agents, and adding 'material' introduces a subjective standard that could allow the company to avoid liability. Since the edited version potentially allows the company to sidestep liability for breaches by representatives that it deems not 'material' which goes against general agency law, it introduces a contradiction.
